category: Large Polyp Stony (LPS) Coral — Button / Doughnut Coral; solitary polyp; monotypic genus

nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reefs and sandy seabeds with moderate water movement; typically found resting on rubble, sand, or low-relief reef areas rather than exposed high-current reef crests

decorPreferences: at least 3 inches spacing from neighboring corals
stable substrate — avoid high-flow areas that can tumble the coral
live rock in vicinity for water quality

diet.notes: Derives nutrition from both symbiotic zooxanthellae (photosynthesis) and active predation. Supplemental target-feeding with meaty foods — mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, chopped seafood, or small chunks of marine fish — is recommended weekly. The coral has a very large gape and can engulf surprisingly large food items. Feeding is best performed in the evening when the polyp is fully expanded; use a pipette or turkey baster to direct food onto the oral disc. Avoid overfeeding — excess uneaten food should be removed to prevent water quality issues.

goodWith: peaceful LPS corals placed at least 15 cm away
avoidWith: other corals placed within sweeper tentacle range (~12 cm at night)
aggressive LPS corals (torch, hammer, frogspawn) without generous spacing
predatory fish that may bite or nip at the polyp (triggers, pufferfish, large wrasses)
high-flow tankmates or flow that prevents full polyp inflation

summary: Cynarina lacrymalis is a large, solitary LPS coral with a distinctive inflated, jewel-like polyp reaching ~13 cm across. Generally considered intermediate care; best placed on the sand bed in low flow with weekly meaty feedings.
careNotes:
Cynarina lacrymalis is a monotypic genus — the only species in genus Cynarina — and is one of the most visually striking LPS corals in the hobby. Its large, fleshy polyp fills with water and can expand to twice its contracted size, displaying vivid translucent coloration ranging from green, red, pink, and brown to rare pastel and patterned morphs.

Key care notes:

• Placement: Best placed on the sand bed or low-lying rubble in the lower third of the aquarium. The coral naturally inhabits sandy substrates and low-relief reef zones, not high-current reef crests. Placing it on rockwork at mid-height exposes it to higher flow than it tolerates and is generally not advised.

• Lighting: Generally thrives under low to moderate PAR (30–100). Sources vary: ReefChasers recommends 100–150 PAR which reflects a moderately-placed specimen, while sand-bed husbandry practice and the species' natural habitat suggest 30–100 PAR as more typical for optimal long-term health. Acclimate new specimens to tank lighting gradually; excessive light can cause bleaching.

• Water flow: Low flow is strongly recommended. High or direct flow will prevent the polyp from fully inflating and can cause tissue damage over time. When the polyp is "deflated" or contracted most of the day, check whether flow is too high.

• Aggression: Extends sweeper tentacles up to approximately 12 cm at night to sting neighboring corals. Maintain at least 15 cm (6 inches) clearance from neighboring corals to avoid chemical warfare or physical contact. Placement on the open sand bed typically provides natural spacing.

• Feeding: Supplement with meaty foods weekly. The large gape allows consumption of surprisingly large prey items — whole small mysis, krill, or chopped seafood are all accepted. Feed in the evening when the polyp is fully inflated for best uptake.

• Care level: Sources disagree — LiveAquaria rates it "easy," while ReefChasers rates it "advanced" (citing its slow growth, sensitivity, wild-collected status making it expensive and irreplaceable). The intermediate rating here reflects the consensus of most reef hobbyist communities: it is not a beginner coral (requires stable parameters and weekly target feeding), but is forgiving of moderate parameter variation compared to SPS.

• Taxonomy note: LiveAquaria and some older sources list this species under family Mussidae. The modern molecular-based classification (Fukami et al. 2008, followed by WoRMS and Wikipedia) places it in family Lobophylliidae, which was split from Mussidae. Lobophylliidae is used here.

• Confusion with Scolymia: Cynarina lacrymalis is frequently confused with Scolymia corals (now classified as Homophyllia) in the aquarium trade. Key distinction: Cynarina has a distinctly inflated, balloon-like polyp with translucent tissue and multiple sets of septa visible; Scolymia/Homophyllia tends to be flatter and less inflated. Cynarina is also a monotypic genus.

• Propagation: Frags poorly due to the single large polyp; primarily wild-collected from the Indo-Pacific. This makes it relatively expensive ($150–$400+ for desirable color morphs) and an investment requiring appropriate care.

• Hermaphrodite: Reproduces sexually by broadcast spawning (eggs and sperm released into the water column), typically in April in the wild. Also capable of asexual reproduction under low-light conditions.
breedingNotes: Cynarina lacrymalis is a hermaphrodite that reproduces sexually via broadcast spawning, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column where fertilization occurs; wild spawning has been documented in April. Asexual reproduction under low-light conditions has also been reported. Captive fragging is generally not feasible due to the solitary single-polyp structure; propagation for the trade is almost exclusively through wild collection.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — Button Coral, Cynarina
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (temperature 72–78°F, salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–12), care level (Easy — averaged down with ReefChasers' Advanced to Intermediate), lighting (Moderate), flow (Low), placement (Bottom to Middle), diet (meaty foods, evening feeding). Family listed as Mussidae; corrected to Lobophylliidae per modern taxonomy.

additional.name: ReefChasers — Cynarina Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked care level (Advanced — averaged with LiveAquaria's Easy to Intermediate per playbook disagreement rule), temperature (77–84°F / 25–29°C — averaged with LiveAquaria to 24–27°C), PAR (100–150), kH (8–9 — averaged with LiveAquaria's 8–12 to 8–11), calcium (400–450 ppm), sweeper tentacles confirmed (extend at night), adult size (up to 6 inches / 15.2 cm), care notes on wild-collection status, fragability, and spacing requirements.

additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family Lobophylliidae, Order Scleractinia), native range (western Indo-Pacific including Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, East Africa, Seychelles, Japan, Indonesia, northern/eastern Australia), depth range (to 40 m), adult size confirmation (10 cm diameter, 8 cm height), reproduction biology (hermaphrodite, broadcast spawning in April, asexual reproduction under low light), and common name disambiguation (flat cup coral, solitary cup coral, button coral, doughnut coral, cat's eye coral).
